title: The Country Where Paddocks Dream Their Own Shape

features.name: the shearing shed that dreams
features.desc-on-touch: Timber weathered to silver; inside, the air still holds the sheep's wool-memory. The shed dreams the paddock's shape in its own wood grain, slowly becoming the landscape it tends.

features.name: the unmarked boundary
features.desc-on-touch: Where the paddock ends, no fence remains. The country has learned to hold its own borders in grass memory, in the depth of soil, in the angle of light at dusk.

features.name: the open well
features.desc-on-touch: Water that has learned to stay still, reflecting not sky but the shape of the paddock it dreams, night after night, until the dream becomes the waking landscape.

features.name: the settler's house, unmade
features.desc-on-touch: A foundation stone, a chimney breast, the memory of a house that learned to become paddock again. The country took it back slowly, the way a river takes a bank.

features.name: the divining rod's resting place
features.desc-on-touch: A crooked stick marking nothing and everything: the spot where someone once tried to read the land, and the land learned to read itself instead.

inscription: the paddock shapes itself each night; by morning the grass holds a new geometry only dreamers know

file.name: Paddock_Survey_Unmapped_1994.txt
file.note: field notes where coordinates begin converting to descriptions; measurements turn to poetry; the surveyor's uncertainty written in margins

exits.label: back eastward to where maps still think they work
westward into country that has learned to dream in layers
northward toward the high paddocks where the shaping grows steeper
southward to the flat where dreaming becomes uniform, almost peaceful
